dependencies: Single supplier ASML for EUV lithography tools critical for Intel 4 and subsequent leading-edge manufacturing process nodes
Limited number of third-party foundries offer leading-edge manufacturing processes, geographically concentrated in Asia
Most advanced current and future products exclusively manufactured by TSMC or reliant upon critical components from TSMC
Many products depend on suppliers in Taiwan for critical components including various compute die that cannot be easily or quickly replaced
Substrates and other components from single or limited suppliers
Concentration of supply chain in certain geographies creates disruption risks
risks: Suppliers extending lead times, experiencing capacity constraints, limiting or canceling supply, allocating supply to competitors, delaying or canceling deliveries, or increasing prices
Cybersecurity events, IP litigation, natural disasters, pandemics, operational failures disrupting suppliers
Long lead times to qualify alternate suppliers or unavailability of qualified alternate suppliers
Supply chain disruptions from macroeconomic and geopolitical conditions including COVID-19 outbreaks
Widespread shortages of substrates and other components
Insufficient foundry capacity preventing meeting customer demand
Concentration risk in Taiwan for critical components and manufacturing
Geopolitical tensions affecting supply chain including US-China trade tensions and Taiwan-mainland China tensions

regulatory_risks: US export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment and AI/advanced computing products to China
Restrictions on sales to certain Chinese technology companies and entities on prohibited lists
Customer allocation restrictions and per-country caps on high-performance integrated circuits
Antitrust laws and restrictions based on cybersecurity and national security concerns
CHIPS Act compliance requirements including restrictions on dividends, change of control transactions, and sharing project returns with US government
Potential repayment of government incentive awards if unable to comply with requirements

Complex and evolving laws across jurisdictions affecting IP, tax, import/export, anti-corruption, foreign exchange, data privacy and localization, competition, advertising, employment, product regulations, environment/health/safety, and consumer laws
Expanding privacy legislation and data-protection compliance costs
Climate-related disclosure requirements including California climate change disclosure requirements, EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive, and SEC climate-change disclosure requirements
Environmental regulations including air quality, wastewater, greenhouse gas emissions, PFAS restrictions
Regulations limiting use of perfluorocarbons and other chemicals critical to semiconductor manufacturing
Forced labor restrictions and responsible minerals sourcing requirements
Evolving AI regulations and industry standards
European Commission competition matters and potential fines
Patent and IP infringement litigation risks
Securities laws compliance including disclosure requirements
Government contract procurement laws and regulations with special rules on accounting, IP rights, expenses, reviews, information handling, security
Trade policies including tariffs, trade barriers, and protectionist measures
Changes in tax laws including global minimum tax under Pillar Two of OECD BEPS initiative

sanction_risks: US export restrictions on products and technology to certain Chinese technology companies
Restrictions on semiconductor manufacturing equipment exports to China
Russia and Belarus sanctions resulting in suspension of operations and no exports to Russia in 2023 and 2024
Currency restrictions and regulations from Russia
Export restrictions may impede ability to provide updates or patches to customers in certain geographies or on sanctions lists
Sanctions lists may include third parties needed for coordinated vulnerability disclosure
Potential for additional trade-related actions and restrictions under new US administration
Risk of China restricting US access to certain minerals in response to US restrictions
Risk of additional Chinese companies being added to prohibited lists
